ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper appears to introduce a notable novel application and combination: claiming the first full-coverage neural semantic graph parser for MRS and a joint model predicting predicates and token alignments (p(t,a|e)) is a clear new contribution. Its use of stack-based embedding features, pointer-network hard attention, and an arc-eager transition-system variant represents a non-trivial extension of existing transition-based and attention methods. However, the core components (transition-based parsing, pointer networks, encoder-decoder models) are established techniques, so the novelty is substantial but not paradigm-shifting.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: it addresses semantic graph parsing (MRS and AMR) using contemporary neural methods (encoder‑decoder transition‑based parser with stack embeddings and pointer networks). It evaluates on standard benchmarks (DeepBank v1.1 and LDC2015E86) using accepted metrics (Smatch, EDM) and compares to relevant baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers), which matches community expectations. The problem formulation, methods, and evaluation standards are appropriate for the venue and the paper is positioned correctly for the semantic parsing/NLP research community.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments are fairly comprehensive: the model is evaluated on DeepBank (MRS) and a standard AMR dataset, compared against multiple baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers, grammar-based and arc-eager baselines) using EDM and Smatch, and runtime advantages are reported. Appropriate ablations/sensitivity checks are present (hard pointer-network vs. soft-attention, alternative linearizations/arc-eager variant) and several failure modes and limitations are discussed (lossy top-down linearization, concept prediction weakness, DeepBank coverage limitation). Related work coverage is described as thorough and hyperparameters are reported, but code availability is not mentioned and the DeepBank corpus coverage constraint limits end-to-end evaluation, so full reproducibility and completeness have minor gaps.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important problem by presenting what it claims is the first full-coverage neural semantic graph parser for MRS, introducing an incremental transition-based model that jointly predicts predicates and token alignments (p(t,a|e)) using stack-based embeddings and pointer-network hard attention. Empirically it outperforms attention baselines on MRS and AMR and is an order of magnitude faster than a high-precision grammar-based parser, achieving a strong 86.69% Smatch, which suggests practical utility and influence. While limitations (lossy reentrancy handling, weaker concept prediction, and evaluation constrained to ERG-acceptable sentences) temper its immediate universality, the approach is likely to change how researchers approach neural graph parsing for linguistically-rich formalisms and to open significant follow-up work.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to a broad set of appropriate baselines (JAMR, CAMR, sequence-to-sequence AMR parsers, a synchronous HRG model, and Damonte et al.'s arc-eager AMR parser) on standard datasets (DeepBank v1.1 and LDC2015E86) using established metrics (Smatch, EDM). Comparisons appear fair—same data and metrics are used, speed is contrasted with a grammar-based parser, and the paper includes method ablations (hard pointer vs soft attention, arc-eager variant). The authors explicitly acknowledge when prior work is comparable or superior (e.g., lagging behind state-of-the-art AMR parsers that use extensive external resources) and discuss known limitations, and related work coverage is described as thorough.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized and presents its contributions early (clear claims about a transition-based neural MRS parser and pointer-network attention). The methodology and experiments are described thoroughly (datasets, baselines, metrics, hyperparameters and limitations are all reported), so notation and definitions come across as generally clear. Figures/tables and examples appear to be effective for comparing results, and overall writing quality is good, with only minor presentation issues implied by the noted limitations and some discussion of lossy linearizations.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence on DeepBank v1.1 and LDC2015E86 with appropriate baselines and metrics (EDM, Smatch), and the model description (transition system, pointer-network attention, stack embeddings) appears technically coherent with hyperparameters reported. There are no obvious mathematical errors reported in the summary. However, methodology has some limitations: evaluation excludes ~15% of sentences the ERG cannot parse, there is no mention of statistical significance or detailed hardware controls for the speed comparisons, and the top-down linearization requires heuristic reentrancy recovery. These caveats mean a few claims (e.g., “first full-coverage” and outperforming an AMR upper-bound) may be overstated, but they do not undermine the main experimental findings.

main_claims: Proposes a neural encoder-decoder transition-based parser that is the first full-coverage semantic graph parser for Minimal Recursion Semantics (MRS).

The model uses stack-based embedding features to predict graphs jointly with unlexicalized predicates and their token alignments, and employs pointer-network hard attention and an arc-eager transition system variant.
The parser is more accurate than attention-based baselines on MRS and on an AMR benchmark, and GPU batch processing makes it an order of magnitude faster than a high-precision grammar-based parser.
Their MRS parser achieves an 86.69% Smatch score, which they state is higher than the upper-bound on AMR parsing.
_summary.methodology: A neural encoder-decoder transition-based parser that incrementally predicts semantic graph derivations and node-to-token alignments (modeling p(t,a|e)), using stack-based embeddings, an arc-eager transition system variant for graph parsing, and pointer-network hard attention (with an alternative soft-attention baseline).
datasets: DeepBank v1.1 (ERG 12141) derived from Penn Treebank WSJ
LDC2015E86 (AMR dataset released for SemEval 2016 AMR parsing Shared Task)
baselines: Sequence-to-sequence AMR parsers (Barzdins and Gosko, 2016; Peng et al., 2017)
Synchronous Hyperedge Replacement Grammar model (Peng and Gildea, 2016)
Damonte et al. (2016) arc-eager AMR parser
limitations: The top-down linearization representation is potentially lossy: reentrancies are represented by repeating the label and alignment of the dependent node and recovered heuristically.
Concept prediction remains a weakness of the model (noted for AMR parsing).

Parses in DeepBank are only provided for sentences for which the ERG has an acceptable analysis, so parsing accuracy cannot be evaluated for sentences the ERG cannot parse (approximately 15% of the original corpus).

The arc-eager model sometimes underperforms other linearizations due to noise in automatically-obtained alignments; and the model still lags behind state-of-the-art AMR parsers that use extensive external resources.
